content: This MRI report provides structural neuroimaging assessment performed using standard clinical acquisition parameters. Structural findings include normal cortical morphology with no evidence of mass lesion, infarction, or hemorrhage. Volumetric analysis demonstrates age-appropriate regional brain volumes. White matter assessment reveals no significant signal abnormalities on FLAIR sequences. The overall impression is of a normal MRI study with moderate confidence. All findings should be interpreted by a qualified radiologist in clinical context.

sections.body: Patient is a 45-year-old individual referred for structural neuroimaging as part of comprehensive neuropsychiatric evaluation. No contraindications to MRI identified during screening. Patient tolerated the procedure well with no claustrophobia or motion-related issues. Clinical indication is evaluation of cognitive complaints with attention difficulties over the past year. No prior neuroimaging on file for comparison. Medical history notable for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. No history of head trauma or neurological conditions.

MRI performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner with 32-channel head coil. Sequences acquired include: (1) 3D T1-weighted MPRAGE with TR=2300ms, TE=2.98ms, TI=900ms, flip angle 9 degrees, voxel size 1x1x1mm isotropic; (2) 2D FLAIR with TR=9000ms, TE=81ms, TI=2500ms, slice thickness 3mm; (3) Diffusion-weighted imaging with b-values 0 and 1000 s/mm2, 30 directions, 2mm isotropic voxels. Total scan time approximately 35 minutes. Quality assessment confirms adequate signal-to-noise ratio across all sequences with no significant motion artifact.

Cortical gray matter demonstrates normal gyral and sulcal patterns with no focal atrophy, heterotopia, or cortical dysplasia identified. No mass lesion, acute infarction, or hemorrhage is seen on any sequence. The ventricles are normal in size and configuration with no midline shift. Hippocampal formations appear symmetric and of normal volume bilaterally. Basal ganglia and thalami demonstrate normal signal intensity and morphology. Cerebellum and brainstem appear normal. No extra-axial collections or calvarial abnormalities. Orbits, sinuses, and mastoids are unremarkable in the visualized portions.

Automated volumetric segmentation using FreeSurfer reveals total brain volume of 1285 mL, within the 45th percentile for age and sex. Hippocampal volumes are 3.8 mL (left) and 3.9 mL (right), both within normal limits. Frontal lobe volume shows no significant asymmetry. Temporal lobe volumes are symmetric and age-appropriate. Caudate and putamen volumes are within expected ranges. Total cortical surface area is 1720 cm2. Mean cortical thickness is 2.5mm, consistent with normative data for this age group. No regional volumes fall outside 2 standard deviations of expected values.

FLAIR sequences demonstrate no focal or confluent white matter signal abnormalities. No periventricular or deep white matter hyperintensities exceeding Fazekas grade 0. Diffusion tensor imaging metrics reveal normal fractional anisotropy in major white matter tracts including the corpus callosum, superior longitudinal fasciculus, and corticospinal tracts bilaterally. Mean diffusivity values are within normal range. No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemic changes. The white matter architecture appears intact and age-appropriate.

Normal brain MRI. No structural abnormality identified on standard clinical sequences. Volumetric analysis demonstrates age-appropriate regional brain volumes with no significant asymmetries or areas of atrophy. White matter appears intact with no evidence of demyelination, ischemic change, or other pathology. These findings carry a moderate confidence level given standard acquisition parameters and automated processing pipelines. Clinical correlation is recommended.

No urgent follow-up imaging is indicated based on current findings. If cognitive symptoms persist or worsen, consider repeat imaging in 12-18 months for longitudinal comparison. Functional MRI or advanced diffusion imaging may provide additional information about network-level connectivity if indicated clinically. Results should be integrated with neuropsychological testing and other clinical assessments for comprehensive evaluation. Consider qEEG as a complementary functional neuroimaging modality.

This report has been reviewed and attested by a board-certified neuroradiologist. All findings represent the professional opinion of the reviewing radiologist based on the imaging data provided. Automated volumetric measurements are used as supplementary data and have been verified against visual assessment.
